19: We will now delve into further examples within the Google ADK framework, with
20: particular emphasis on hierarchical, parallel, and sequential coordination paradigms,
21: alongside the implementation of an agent as an operational instrument.
23: The following code example demonstrates the establishment of a hierarchical agent
24: structure within the Google ADK through the creation of a parent-child relationship.
25: The code defines two types of agents: LlmAgent and a custom TaskExecutor agent
26: derived from BaseAgent. The TaskExecutor is designed for specific, non-LLM tasks
27: and in this example, it simply yields a "Task finished successfully" event. An LlmAgent
28: named greeter is initialized with a specified model and instruction to act as a friendly
29: greeter. The custom TaskExecutor is instantiated as task_doer. A parent LlmAgent
30: called coordinator is created, also with a model and instructions. The coordinator's
31: instructions guide it to delegate greetings to the greeter and task execution to the
32: task_doer. The greeter and task_doer are added as sub-agents to the coordinator,
33: establishing a parent-child relationship. The code then asserts that this relationship is
34: correctly set up. Finally, it prints a message indicating that the agent hierarchy has
